coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic tetracycline degradation using N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr composites: Unraveling the complementary effects between N-CQDs and oxygen vacancy
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Hanbo
Huang, Jinhui
Jiang, Longbo
Shi, Yahui
Yi, Kaixin
Zhang, Wei
Zhang, Jin
Chen, Haoyun
Yuan, Xingzhong
coredata.dc:description: Complementary effect of components in a hybrid photocatalyst is an essential factor for improving solar energy-conversion efficiency, yet the underlying correlation between nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and oxygen vacancy (OV) is still ambiguous. Herein, using BiOBr nanosheets as model photocatalyst, we studied the specific roles of N-CQDs and OV in antibiotic photodegradation over full-spectrum. Electrons were overwhelmingly collected on N-CQDs rather than OV, achieving prominent separation of photo-excitons. However, OV could extend the congenitally optical absorption boundary of BiOBr to maximum utilizing the up-converted photons by N-CQDs. Moreover, active radical tests revealed that N-CQDs mainly regulated the single-electron reduction of dissolved oxygen to produce superoxide radicals (O2 −) and OV mainly drove the robust H2O2 generation via two-electron reduction of the chemisorbed oxygen molecules. Although partial O2 − were consumed as the raw of H2O2, a Photo-Fenton like reaction could transform H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (OH) to provide more powerful oxidizing force. This work provides an in-depth understanding on the complementary roles of N-CQDs and OV and is helpful for designing highly efficient metallic oxide catalysts for photocatalytic pollutant removal.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Oxygen vacancy
BiOBr
Complementary effect
Oxygen activation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of pristine BiOBr and OV-BiOBr
Effects of various practical conditions on photocatalytic process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic activities of different photocatalysts under (a) UV light, (b) visible light and (c) NIR light irradiation and the corresponding reaction dynamic data (d), (e) and (f).
Effects of (a) initial solution pH, (b) initial TC concentration, (c) coexisting ions and (d) water sources on TC photodegradation performance by NOB-6 under visible light irradiation.
The repeated photocatalytic experiments of NOB-6 photocatalyst for degradation of TC under visible light irradiation.
(a, b) Photocatalytic activities with different scavengers for the degradation of TC and the corresponding photocatalytic removal efficiency over NOB-6 heterojunction under visible light irradiation.
DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra in NOB-6 system: (a) DMPO-O2 − and (b) DMPO-OH.
(a) Time-dependence of the NBT decomposition over the samples; (b) time-dependent concentration plots of O2 −; (c) production and (d) decomposition of H2O2 under visible light irradiation.
Proposed mechanism for TC degradation on NOB-6 under full-spectrum light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic tetracycline degradation using N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr composites: Unraveling the complementary effects between N-CQDs and oxygen vacancy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control, Hunan University, Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environment Biology and Pollution Control, Hunan University, Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Complementary effect of components in a hybrid photocatalyst is an essential factor for improving solar energy-conversion efficiency, yet the underlying correlation between nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and oxygen vacancy (OV) is still ambiguous. Herein, using BiOBr nanosheets as model photocatalyst, we studied the specific roles of N-CQDs and OV in antibiotic photodegradation over full-spectrum. Electrons were overwhelmingly collected on N-CQDs rather than OV, achieving prominent separation of photo-excitons. However, OV could extend the congenitally optical absorption boundary of BiOBr to maximum utilizing the up-converted photons by N-CQDs. Moreover, active radical tests revealed that N-CQDs mainly regulated the single-electron reduction of dissolved oxygen to produce superoxide radicals (O2 −) and OV mainly drove the robust H2O2 generation via two-electron reduction of the chemisorbed oxygen molecules. Although partial O2 − were consumed as the raw of H2O2, a Photo-Fenton like reaction could transform H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (OH) to provide more powerful oxidizing force. This work provides an in-depth understanding on the complementary roles of N-CQDs and OV and is helpful for designing highly efficient metallic oxide catalysts for photocatalytic pollutant removal.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, a great deal of poisonous and hazardous pollutants is discharged due to the persistent population growth and industrial production, which has become a serious environmental issue in many countries [1]. In response to these imminent pollution concerns, many robust approaches have stand out over the past few years to wipe out the detrimental components from various wastewaters, such as adsorption [2], photocatalysis [3], electrolysis [4], membranes separation [5,6] and Fenton reaction [7]. Among various environmental purification projects, semiconductor photocatalysis technology, an efficient and cost-effective strategy to obtain chemical energy with solar energy as the external driving force, is granted a place for providing a viably long-term solution with enormous potential to address the environmental pollution and energy crisis [1,8]. Since the landmark report of photocatalytic water splitting on TiO2 electrode [9], massive research attention has been devoted to the exploitation of impressive photocatalysts, for instance g-C3N4, MoS2, CdS, SrTiO3, Bi2MoO6, etc [10-15].

Aurivillius-phase bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) compound semiconductor have garnered considerable attention in the arena of solar energy conversion due to its remarkable merits, including environmental friendliness, relatively inexpensiveness as well as optical and chemical stability [16]. Inherently, the conduction band (CB) of BiOBr is mainly occupied by Bi 6p state, while the valence band (VB) is predominated by Br 4p state with significant hybridization with O 2p and Bi 6 s orbitals [17]. This provides a prerequisite condition for BiOBr to be excited under UV- and visible-light irradiation. Moreover, the only p-block elements (Bi, Br and O) could also promote the formation of highly dispersive energy band structures with low reciprocal effective mass of photoinduced electrons (e−), thereby benefitting the charge carrier transfer [18]. As previous reported, BiOBr exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than TiO2 under identical conditions due to its layered structures and wider optical absorption region [19]. However, there are some demerits that still cannot be ignored, for instance, the narrow optical absorption edge (around 435 nm) of BiOBr hinders its capacity to maximize the solar-to-chemical energy conversion virtue. Besides, the bare surface with scarce active sites leads to sluggish surface reaction kinetics, which further results in serious recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [20]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a facile scheme to solve the existing problems.

Recently, Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and oxygen vacancy (OV) have been identified as the promising approaches to prolong the lifetime of charge carriers, widen the optical window and even boom the photocatalytic ability [18,21]. As a kind of carbon nanomaterials, N-CQDs exhibit eminent charge transfer capability to suppress the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [22,23]. Also, more and more scholars have found the up-conversion effect of N-CQDs and applied them in long-wavelength light response [24,25]. Similarly, OV has been regarded as a trusty recipe in tuning optical property [26], which is able to induce the generation of isolated level below the conduction band, thus leading to the increment of solar light harvesting via providing shallow donors. Besides, the OV can also serve as a surface separation center to trap charge carriers, retarding the recombination of e−-h+ pairs and promoting the utilization efficiency of surface-arrived excitons [27]. Furthermore, it has been reported that both N-CQDs and OV were able to serve as the active sites for oxygen activation [28-30]. For example, Aschauer and his co-workers reported that the OV on the surface of TiO2 could react with the adsorbed O2 to produce interstitial reactive oxygen species [31]. Zhu et al. synthesized a carbon-supported oxygen vacancy-rich Co3O4 photocatalyst to robustly produce hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [32]. Ji et al. demonstrated that N-CQDs modification could tune the concentration of reactive oxygen species [22]. However, single modification could only activate molecule oxygen through a single way, either reducing O2 with one electron to generate superoxide radicals (O2 −) or transforming O2 with two electrons to produce H2O2 which further decomposes into hydroxyl radicals (OH). Therefore, in order to maximize the photocatalytic performance, it is urgent to enhance the yield of both O2 − and H2O2, simultaneously. Moreover, simply introducing N-CQDs (OV) could only contribute finitely to the expansion of optical window. Considering the positive effects of N-CQDs and OV, a cooperation of them should be suitable for more striking photocatalytic performance. Although there have been many investigations focusing on the correlation between N-CQDs (OV) and BiOBr, limited information is available about the complementary effects of N-CQDs and OV in optical property and free radical production.

Herein, we first synchronously loaded N-CQDs and oxygen vacancies (OV) on the surface of BiOBr to achieve the efficient degradation of tetracycline (TC) under full-spectrum light illumination without involving any noble metals and rare earth elements. The roles of N-CQDs and OV are systemically discussed through density functional theory (DFT) calculation and various control experiments. Based on the results of superoxide radical detection, optical absorption and photoelectrochemical properties analysis, the possible degradation mechanism of TC over N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr photocatalyst was proposed. Moreover, some actual conditions are simulated to assess the potential of N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr composites in the real application. The synthetic strategy presented here provides a blanket way for metallic oxides to synergistically optimize the optical absorption, charge transfer, radical generation and surface reactivity.

The N-CQDs aqueous solution was synthesized via a hydrothermal method according to the previously reported literature with minor modifications [33]. Typically, 10 mmol ammonium citrate was dissolved into 20 mL ultrapure water, which contained 670 μL ethylenediamine. After 30 min stirring, the homogeneous solution was poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. Eventually, the obtained black liquid was subjected to dialysis (MWCO 1000) for one day to obtain the N-CQDs solution. As comparison, undoped CQDs solution was synthesized by the same procedure, except that ammonium citrate was replaced with sodium citrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pristine BiOBr and OV-BiOBr
ce:section.ce:para: OV-BiOBr photocatalysts were fabricated using a hydrothermal method [34]. 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 20 mL of solution comprising 3 mL concentrated nitric acid and 17 mL ultrapure water with stirring for 15 min. Meanwhile, 3 mmol NH4Br was dissolved into 10 mL ultrapure water. After stirring for another 15 min, the above two solutions were mixed quickly and milky suspension was obtained. Subsequently, 8 mL absolute alcohol was dropped into the above suspension, and the mixtures were transferred into 50 mL autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. The resulting precipitate was washed and dried for further use. The pristine BiOBr was also prepared by the same process without the addition of absolute alcohol.

N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr were synthesized by a modified one-pot hydrothermal synthesis method [34]. Specifically, 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 20 mL of solution comprising 3 mL concentrated nitric acid and 17 mL ultrapure water with stirring for 15 min. Meanwhile, 3 mmol NH4Br and a certain volume of N-CQDs aqueous solution were dissolved into 10 mL ultrapure water. After stirring for another 15 min, the above two solutions were mixed quickly with continuous stirring until the coffee-like suspension was obtained. Subsequently, 8 mL absolute alcohol was dropped into the above suspension, and then the mixtures were transferred into 50 mL autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. Production was obtained through centrifugation, washing with water and ethanol. The resulting production dried at 70 °C overnight. The samples were labeled as NOB-X (X is the volume of N-CQDs solution). They were NOB-2, NOB-4, NOB-6, NOB-8, respectively. For comparison, the 6 mL N-CQDs/BiOBr (denoted as NB-6) was also synthesized via the same method without adding absolute alcohol. Also, the 6 mL CQDs/OV-BiOBr was synthesized by replacing N-CQDs solution with CQDs solution, labeled as COB-6.

The photoactivities were evaluated by the photo-degradation of TC under vis- and NIR-light irradiation, respectively. In a typical procedure, 50 mg photocatalyst was dispersed into a quartz vessel containing 100 mL TC solution at a concentration of 20 mg L−1. Visible light was provided by a 300 W Xe lamp with a 400 nm cutoff filter, and NIR-light was provided by a 760 nm cutoff filter. Before the photocatalytic experiments start, the suspension was kept stirring in the dark for 1 h to achieve an adsorption–desorption equilibrium. At a predetermined time interval, 4 mL suspension was withdrawn and filtrated through 0.45 µm Millipore filters for further analysis. The photo-degradation efficiency of TC was measured with an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2250, SHIMADZU Corporation, Japan) at the characteristic absorption wavelength of 357 nm.

For evaluating the contribution of active species (O2 −) yielded in photocatalytic TC degradation process, nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) was chosen as a O2 − scavenger, according to the fact that NBT could be reduced by O2 − and formed insoluble purple formazan as shown in Scheme S1 [35,36]. The procedure resembled the photocatalytic performance tests, in which 20 mg L−1 TC was replaced with 0.015 mM NBT and the concentration of O2 − was quantitatively analyzed with a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer at 260 nm. Due to one NBT molecules could react with four O2 − radicals, we could obtain the yield (molar concentration) of O2 − radicals in the photocatalytic system via multiplying the reduction of molar concentration in NBT by 4.

The photocatalytic H2O2 production and decomposition experiments were both evaluated under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm). For the H2O2 production test, 0.05 g of catalyst was dispersed into 0.1 L of IPA solution (10 vol%) in a container with stirring in the dark for 1 h. After illumination with a 300 W Xe lamp for a period of time, 3 mL of suspension was withdrawn and filtrated with 0.45 µm Millipore filter. The H2O2 decomposition behavior was measured by dispersing 100 mg of sample in 100 mL of 0.15 mmol L−1 H2O2 solution and irradiating for 60 min. At appropriate time intervals, 3 mL suspension was collected and filtrated. The amount of H2O2 was quantified by an iodometric assay [37]. Typically, 1 mL 0.1 M C8H5KO4 aqueous solution and 1 mL 0.4 M KI aqueous solution were added to the filtered solution keeping for 0.5 h. The H2O2 molecules will react with iodide anions (I−) under acidic conditions to generate triiodide anions (I3 −), which could be monitored at 350 nm by a spectrophotometer.

Uniform N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr composites were synthesized through a modified hydrothermal method. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns indicated the formation of the target materials without detectable impurities (Fig. S1). The oxygen vacancies (OV) were mainly located on the (001) facets of BiOBr. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was implemented to investigate the surface elemental composition as well as the chemical states (Fig. S2). The slight shift of Bi 4f peaks in NOB-6 indicated the appearance of interaction between the BiOBr and N-CQDs [21], as well as the existence of OV due to charge compensation [38]. Meanwhile, the intensity of O at 531.17 eV rose slightly due to the chemisorbed oxygen species, implying the appearance of OV [39]. The existence of C 1 s and N 1 s spectra further confirmed the co-existence of N-doped CQDs and BiOBr with surface oxygen vacancies in the N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr materials. Moreover, the electron spin resonance (ESR) provided a solid evidence for the successful introduction of oxygen-deficient defects on BiOBr surface, where a remarkable signal at around g = 2.002 appeared compared with pristine BiOBr (Fig. S3) [40]. More detailed XRD, XPS, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman Spectroscopy, Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) characterization results of the as-prepared samples were provided in the Supplementary information.

UV–vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy technique showed that pure BiOBr exhibited poor photoabsorption in the visible-light region with a threshold at about 435 nm (Fig S7). After incorporating N-CQDs and OV onto BiOBr surface, the light harvesting capacity of the resulting hybrid from 200 nm to 1100 nm had a significant improvement, highlighting the positive effects of OV and N-CQDs on the optical absorption properties of BiOBr. Correspondingly, from the transformed Kubelka-Munk (KM) function (Fig. S9), the band gap of the modified BiOBr composites were narrowed compared with pure BiOBr. All these observations suggested the potential of N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr for visible-NIR-light redox catalysis.

In order to evaluate the photocurrent response and transfer property of charge carriers, the photoelectric current (PC) and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was implemented. As shown in Fig. S12b, NOB-6 exhibited the highest photocurrent density, which was about threefold as much as that of pristine BiOBr. Moreover, it also possessed the smallest EIS arc radius (Fig. S12d), indicating the best electronic conductivity. The normalized transient decay profile of open circuit potential (OCP) demonstrated that the obtained recombination rate constant of NOB-6 (2.94 × 10−2 s−1) was only about 9.8% of pure BiOBr (3.04 × 10−1 s−1) (Fig. S12a). These results incontrovertibly validated that the incorporation of surface oxygen vacancies and N-CQDs on BiOBr could simultaneously endow the resultant hybrid with high-efficiency charge transfer and ultrafast charge separation, thereby ensuring more sufficient photoexciton furnishing for the further interfacial degradation reaction. To further analyze the optical response ability and charge transfer performance, the steady-state photoluminescence spectra (PL), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), the photocurrent density under NIR light irradiation and density functional theory (DFT) calculation were also carried out and listed in Supplementary material.

The wide-spectrum photocatalytic activities of the as-fabricated N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr samples were evaluated by the degradation of TC under UV light, visible light and NIR-light irradiation, respectively. Fig. 1 a illustrated that all of the prepared photocatalysts had excellent TC degradation performances under UV light irradiation. About 72.47% ± 2.77% of TC was removed within 60 min over pure BiOBr, and it was improved to 79.52% ± 2.94% after the generation of OV on the surface of BiOBr. The NB-6 and NOB-x photocatalysts had approximate performance and the highest degradation efficiency was improved to 89.42% ± 0.83%. All of the errors were within acceptable limits (5%), demonstrating that the degradation efficiencies were credible and our results were replicable. Although the UV light absorption ability of OV-BiOBr was higher than NB-6 obtained from DRS spectra, the TC degradation over OV-BiOBr under UV light irradiation was weaker than NB-6, which was due to the fact that the electron trapping ability of OV was weaker than N-CQDs, consistent with the PC and PL analysis results.

The photodegradation experiments were further proceeded under visible light irradiation, in which the degradation efficiencies entirely decreased to different extent resulting from the lower energy of visible light. As presented in Fig. 1b, the removal of TC for pure BiOBr was only 46.26% ± 1.48% within 60 min, which might result from the fast recombination of e−-h+ pairs (3.04 × 10−1 s−1) and the low utilization efficiency of visible light over pure BiOBr, as illustrated by optical and electrical tests (Supplementary material). When the OV was built on the surface of BiOBr, the photocatalytic activity was improved to 64.45% ± 2.05%. The enhanced photocatalytic activity could be caused by the formation of oxygen-deficiency-induced defect state that increased visible light adsorption and suppressed the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [41,42]. In detail, OV with abundant localized electrons could create defect states between the CB and VB of BiOBr. When exposed to light irradiation, these localized electrons would be directly excited to the CB and also the inherent electrons in VB could also be trapped during transition, thereby prolonging the lifetime of photogenerated charges and producing more reactive radicals [43]. Interestingly, it was found that the gap of degradation rate between NB-6 (66.52% ± 1.62%) and OV-BiOBr (64.45% ± 2.05%) was narrowed, compared with that under UV light irradiation. Considering the weaker charge separation ability of OV and the more expansive light absorption region, we had enough evidence to conclude that the main function of OV was tuning the optical property of BiOBr rather than separating photogenerated charge carriers. For the N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr photocatalysts, the TC degradation efficiencies were 73.24% ± 2.60%, 75.87% ± 0.83%, 82.68% ± 1.56% and 80.07% ± 0.85% for NOB-2, NOB-4, NOB-6 and NOB-8, respectively. It was distinct that the photocatalytic performance was enhanced significantly and the degradation efficiency was ascending with increasing the amount of N-CQDs. The optimal NOB-x sample with the most suitable N-CQDs loads was NOB-6 with the highest TC elimination activity of 82.68% after one-hour visible light irradiation. However, an excessive content of N-CQDs tended to act as a recombination center, shielding the OV-BiOBr from absorbing light and block the electron pathways (Fig. S15). As a result, the photocatalytic performance was restricted (80.07% vs. 82.68%).

The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of TC were investigated by the pseudo-first-order kinetics model:
(1)
- l n
C /
C 0
= k t
where C0 (mg L−1) and C (mg L−1) are the organic pollutants concentration at t = 0 and each given time, respectively. Moreover, k is the apparent first-order rate constant (min−1). As shown in Fig. 1d and e, the k values of different samples exhibited a rise first followed by a decline with increasing the content of N-CQDs (0.0218 and 0.0099, 0.0263 and 0.0163, 0.0284 and 0.0175, 0.0291 and 0.0210, 0.0320 and 0.0217, 0.0337 and 0.0246, 0.0294 and 0.0223 under UV and visible light irradiation for BiOBr, OV-BiOBr, NB-6, NOB-2, NOB-4, NOB-6 and NOB-8, respectively). The NOB-6 displayed the highest rate constant under both UV light (0.0337 min−1) and visible light (0.0294 min−1) irradiation, which was 1.5 and 2.9 times higher than those of pure BiOBr (0.0218 min−1 for UV light and 0.0099 min−1 for visible light), respectively.

It is well known that the NIR light occupies a great part of solar light. Therefore, we also investigated the photocatalytic activities of different photocatalysts for TC decomposition under NIR light irradiation (λ > 760 nm). The pure BiOBr could hardly remove TC under NIR light, and the concentration was even increased by 0.06% because of desorption of TC from BiOBr surface (Fig. S16). As shown in Fig. 1c, the prepared OV-BiOBr (5.96% ± 0.12%) and NB-6 (5.75% ± 0.16%) sample displayed elevated degradation efficiency than pristine BiOBr under NIR light irradiation, benefitting from the upconversion effect of N-CQDs and the strong tail light absorption induced by OV. It was interesting that the photo-degradation efficiency under NIR light irradiation was increased by almost 12% after the N-CQDs and OV co-modifying BiOBr compared with that of pure BiOBr. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 1f, the apparent kinetic rate was also almost two times higher than those of OV-BiOBr and NB-6. The preeminent performance under NIR light was mainly due to the great cooperation between N-CQDs and oxygen vacancy. To be specific, the N-CQDs converted the near-infrared emission wavelength with lower energy to visible light at wavelength of 400 nm to 600 nm as clarified in up-converted PL spectra. Nevertheless, pure BiOBr was incapable of making full use of the emitted energy due to the narrow absorbing boundary of only 435 nm, resulting in a poor performance under NIR light irradiation. With the OV introduced into, the light absorption range was extended to above 2000 nm so that the up-converted emissions could be fully absorbed by OV-BiOBr. These results cogently indicated that synergistic effect of N-CQDs and oxygen vacancies played pivotal roles for the remarkable improvement of photocatalytic activity under full-spectrum light irradiation. The comparisons of photocatalytic activities of the N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr composite with various photocatalysts previously reported for the photocatalytic degradation of TC were listed in Table S3. As can be seen, the TC removal rate of NOB-6 composite was preponderant, which achieved relatively higher performance with lower cost and shorter time.

The total organic carbon (TOC) removal efficiency was measured to evaluate the mineralization ability of photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. S18, after 1 h of visible light irradiation, TOC removal rates of TC for pure BiOBr, OV-BiOBr, NB-6 and NOB-6 were 6.74%, 19.36%, 18.53% and 27.69%, respectively. The experimental results indicated that the prepared N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr photocatalyst showed much higher mineralization ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of various practical conditions on photocatalytic process
ce:section.ce:para: In the practical wastewaters, the pH value may vary enormously and threaten the photocatalytic activity. Degradation efficiency of TC for NOB-6 with different initial pH values was exhibited in Fig. 2 a. The original pH of TC solution was 4.3. When adjusting the pH to 3 with diluted HNO3 solution, the photocatalytic activity had a weak decrease (from 82.68% to 76.97%). Also, it was observed that the degradation efficiency decreased when initial pH increased (with TC degradation efficiencies of 79.19% and 76.09% for pH 5 and pH 7, respectively), while the efficiency dropped dramatically with initial pH increasing from 9 to 11 (about 10.98% and 12.95% decline for pH 9 and pH 11, respectively), indicating that the alkaline environment brought a negative effort on the photocatalytic process. Although strongly acidic and alkaline condition may cause the disassembly of BiOBr, there was no denying that the transformation of tetracycline speciation and the surface charge of the photocatalyst were dependent on pH level. The TC molecules could form three molecular species referring to cationic species (pH < 3.3), zwitterionic species (3.3 < pH < 7.68) and anionic species (pH > 7.68) [1]. Therefore, the zeta potential of the NOB-6 composite under various pH values was tested and the result was displayed in Table S1. The results showed that the NOB-6 composite had negative surface charge at pH 5–11 so that the electrostatic repulsion with TC species was augmented. Especially, the electronegativity presented a leap when pH value was greater than seven, correspondingly, the photocatalytic ability decreased intensely. However, the NOB-6 had positive charge at pH 3, indicating that the isoelectric point was located between pH 3 and pH 5. Therefore, it was merited that the reaction system at original pH (4.3) exerted the maximum degradation efficiency.

Fig. 2b showed the influence of initial TC concentrations on the photocatalytic activity. It could be detected that the photodegradation efficiency decreased (from 82.68% to 63.97%) accordingly when TC concentrations increased (from 20 to 60 mg L−1). Higher initial pollutant concentrations could bring negative effect in the photocatalytic process, which could be attributed to the possible reason that there were more TC molecules to be decomposed, while the amount of reactive sites and the rate of radical generation were limited. In detail, the photocatalytic production of radicals has its maximum reaction rate when the amount of photocatalyst is constant. In the saturated state, same amount of TC could be decomposed in per unit time. Moreover, the increased TC may compete for the adsorption sites on photocatalyst surface with molecular oxygen resulting in a lower yield of radicals. On the other hand, although the degradation rate of TC decreased at increased concentration of TC, the degradation capacity over per gram of photocatalyst was increased as shown in Fig. S19. It meant that the concentration of intermediates was higher than that at lower TC concentration. Due to the fact that the reaction followed pseudo-first-order kinetics, the production of intermediates and the degradation of TC should keep constant at each TC concentration. These intermediates might consume constant reactive sites and reactive species during each reaction, and the consumption was increased with TC concentration rising. Thus, competition between TC and intermediates for reactive sites and reactive radicals also existed. When the concentration of TC increased, the available reactive sites and reactive radicals for TC were decreased but relatively constant at any time. All in all, the limited amount of reactive sites on photocatalyst and the limited rate of radical production in the photocatalytic system are the roots of this issue. According to our experiments, the suitable concentration of TC should be controlled below 30 mg/L.

As we know, many coexisting inorganic salts existing in the real wastewater may have impacts on the catalytic property [44]. So some general inorganic ions such as Na+, Cl−, SO4 2−, and CO3 2−, were added to the TC solution to explore their influences on the degradation process. Fig. 2c displayed the degradation curves of TC under three model inorganic salts (NaCl, Na2SO4 and Na2CO3) with the concentration of 0.1 mol L−1 under visible light irradiation. It could be observed that NaCl had little influence on TC removal during the photocatalytic process due to the neutral property of NaCl solution. An appropriate amount of Na+ ions and Cl− ions in the water sample would not change the reaction environment and the photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr would still remain a high level. The introduction of SO4 2− inhibited the degradation of TC slightly, which might be ascribed to the fact that SO4 2− ions would compete for the reaction sites with TC molecules [45]. Furthermore, the presence of CO3 2− significantly suppressed the degradation process. Two aspects could be responsible for the phenomenon. On one hand, Na2CO3 could be hydrolyzed to produce a certain amount of OH− ions and HCO3 − ions resulting in the increase of pH value from 4.3 to 10.6. As can be seen in Fig. 2a, the alkaline condition significantly restricted the photocatalytic degradation of TC over N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr photocatalysts. On the other hand, CO3 2− and HCO3 − were effective scavengers towards hydroxyl radicals (OH), reducing the amount of active radicals. As previous reported, both CO3 2− and HCO3 − could react with OH to form less reactive CO3
− radicals, with reactive processes those CO3 2− + OH → CO3
− + OH− (kCO3 2− = 3.9 × 108 M−1 s−1) and HCO3 − + OH → CO3
− + H2O (kHCO3 − = 8.6 × 106 M−1 s−1) [46,47]. Generally, OH radicals could react with many pharmaceuticals rapidly (k = 108 –1010 M−1 s−1). However, CO3
− radicals always possessed relatively lower oxidation potential and higher selectivity [48,49].

Taking the actual application into consideration, the different water sources will greatly affect the photocatalytic performance. The effects of various water sources on the degradation of TC were displayed in Fig. 2d. There was various degree of removal efficiency reduction observed in tap water, river water and underground water, which were 63.42%, 63.91% and 79.03%, respectively. However, the removal efficiency in deionized water was 82.68%. The basic characteristics of different water matrice were listed in Table S2. Accordingly, the pH values of river water and tap water were around 7.3, which was disadvantageous for TC degradation over NOB-6 photocatalyst. Whereas, the fatal factor was the high concentration of SO4 2− and Cl− in river water and tap water. With respect to underground water, the pH value of 5.86 made a little influence to the degradation process. Meanwhile, the relatively negligible existence of inorganic ions only affected the initial phase during degradation. Thus, the removal efficiency in underground water only decreased 3.65% compared to that in deionized water. It was noteworthy that although the removal efficiencies in the practical samples were slightly lower than that in deionized water because of the existence of various competitive substances, the photocatalysts were still very efficient for the removal of TC.

In practice, the good photostability of the as-prepared catalyst are conducive to reducing water treatment costs and avoiding secondary pollution. To test the stability of the N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr composites, the photocatalytic performance for TC degradation of NOB-6 sample after five cycles was investigated. Each experiment lasted 60 min. As shown in Fig. 3 , there is no palpable reduction for TC removal (from 82.68% to 76.23%) under visible light irradiation even after five successive recycles. It indicated that our photocatalyst possessed high stability for its practical application. Besides, the XRD and XPS patterns of the used NOB-6 after five cycles of photodegradation experiments were shown in Fig. S20 and S21, respectively. It could be seen that there was no any obvious distinction between the original one and the used one, implying the phase and structure did not change after photocatalysis reaction. The solubility with pH value was also tested by ICP-MS as shown in Fig. S21e. In most conditions, the solubility of NOB-6 was less than 1 ng/mL except in pH 3. However, the leaching amount of 5.37 ng/mL still had insignificant influence on environment. On the basis of above results, N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr photocatalyst is stable enough for the realistic environment remediation.

In order to uncover the predominant active species formed in the photocatalytic degradation process, triethanolamine (TEOA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were employed as the quenchers of holes (h+), superoxide radical (O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (OH), respectively [50,51]. TEOA was a common sacrificial agent to trap holes in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [52]. As shown in Fig. S23 and S24, BQ and IPA could react with O2 − (k = 9.6 × 108 M−1 s−1) [53] and OH (k = 3 × 109 M−1 s−1) [54] with high speeds, respectively. The generated BQ radical formation (BQ− ) and IPA radical formation (IPA− ) would inhibit the participation of O2 − and OH in TC destruction in oxygenated media [55]. The addition dosage of these quenchers was 1 mM, which could efficiently extinguish the corresponding active species in the pollutant photodegradation process, and the removal efficiency thus became lower. As shown in Fig. 4 , the TC decomposition was remarkably inhibited after the addition of BQ, with the efficiency drastically reduced from 82.68% to 30.67%, indicating that O2 − played an important role in the whole removal process. Similarly, the photocatalytic efficiency of TC dropped to 70.03% when TEOA was added, suggesting that the h+ was another major reaction species for the TC degradation. Note that there was a slight decrease in the removal rate of TC with the presence of IPA, indicating that OH was not a major active species during the degradation of TC. Actually, radical reactions are complicated. O2 − radicals and OH radicals can transform to each other with H2O2 as medium. Previous reports have claimed that OH radicals could transform to O2 − radicals via H2O2 + OH →O2 − + H2O + H+ (k = 3 × 107 M−1 s−1) [56]. However, the reaction rate constant of IPA with OH radicals was 3 × 109 M−1 s−1, which was much faster than that of reaction between H2O2 and OH. If most of O2 − radicals were generated using OH radicals as raw materials, the TC degradation should be significantly restrained in the presence of IPA. However, there was only a weak decline, indicating that most of the O2 − radicals in this system were generated from the reduction of molecule O2 by electrons not from OH. Therefore, we could draw a conclusion that the photocatalytic degradation of TC was primarily driven by the O2 − and h+ species.

In order to further detect the generation of O2 − and OH radicals in the NOB-6 photocatalytic reaction system under visible light, the electron spin resonance (ESR) instrument was implemented. DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide) is a kind of spin traps, which can combine with free radicals (e.g. superoxide radical and hydroxyl radical) to form various relatively stable spin adducts (DMPO-O2 − spin adduct and DMPO-OH spin adduct) [57]. All the spin-trap experiments were carried out under dark condition and visible light irradiation of 5 min and 10 min, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, six clear characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2 − were observed in methanol solution under visible light illumination, which meant that the O2 − radical species were produced from the reduction between photogenerated e− and O2 [58]. It is reasonable for the generation of O2 − in NOB-6 system as the N-CQDs and OVs could greatly enhance the molecular oxygen activation ability according to previous reports [21,59]. Interestingly, the clear signals of DMPO-OH with intensities of 1:2:2:1 were also observed (Fig. 5b), suggesting that the OH species were also generated in NOB-6 system. The results of ESR test were well consistent with that of the free radical trapping experiments.

Upon gathering the above information, we could conclude that the O2 activation was a crucial step to the entire reaction process. Here the molecular O2 activation property was first quantified by using NBT as a probe molecule. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a and S26, the continuous attenuation in absorbance clearly indicated that all samples could generate O2 − under visible light irradiation. As expected, BiOBr possessed the lowest degradation efficiency for NBT in accordance with its poorest TC degradation. Meanwhile, the NBT reduction experiments were also conducted under UV/H2O2 condition and vacuum condition, respectively (Fig. S27), which indicated that OH and h+ imposed negligible effect on NBT reduction. A curious phenomenon was observed that the NBT degradation rates for NB-6, NOB-6 and OV-BiOBr photocatalysts exhibited a descending order, which was contradictory with their TC degradation performances. According to the conversion relation between O2 − and NBT of 4:1 in molar ratio, the total O2 − productions of BiOBr, OV-BiOBr, NB-6 and NOB-6 samples were estimated to be 11.25, 13.12, 24.72 and 17.18 µM after visible light irradiation for 1 h (Fig. 6b). This result demonstrated that N-CQDs and OV could promote the generation of O2 −, but there were further conversions existing in the whole photocatalysis process because the coexistence of N-CQDs and OV produced less O2 − than NB-6. To have an insight into the role of N-CQDs and OV in the oxygen activation, the H2O2 evolution ability under visible light irradiation was investigated and shown in Fig. 6c. Interestingly and importantly, the sample with relatively low O2 − production behaved conspicuous H2O2 evolution performance and the NOB-6 possessed the optimal H2O2 output of 24.44 µM in 60 min. In sharp contrast, the amount of H2O2 produced on NB-6 was only 4.54 µM, which was negligibly higher than BiOBr (4.32 µM). This result indicated that the O2 − was involved in the photocatalytic H2O2 production upon the OV modified photocatalysts (OV-BiOBr and NOB-6), and the incorporation of N-CQDs onto BiOBr could trigger the single-electron O2 reduction (O2 + e− → O2 −) while the OV herein mainly induced the two-electron reduction of O2 (O2 + 2H+ + 2e− → H2O2) [56]. Given that both of the photoinduced e− and the N-CQDs possessing abundant surface ketonic groups and carboxylic groups, enabled the heterolytic H2O2 dissociation to generate OH (H2O2 + e− → OH + OH−) [60,61], the H2O2 decomposition experiments under visible light irradiation were executed with an initial H2O2 concentration of 150 µM. As exhibited in Fig. 6d, the H2O2 decomposed about 36.55% after 60 min of visible light irradiation. This Photo-Fenton like reaction ensured affluently extra OH supply for the photocatalytic degradation of TC.

Therefore, on the basis of the above characterization analysis results and photocatalysis experiments, the reaction mechanism of the prepared N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr composites is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 7 . Under the UV and visible light illumination, pure BiOBr can be excited by absorbing the light with wavalengths of less than 435 nm. Since the low charge separation efficiency and the insufficient optical utilization capacity, the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr is restricted. With the formation of NOB composites, the OV-induced mid-gap electronic states act as springboards for the transition of photoinduced e−, enlarging the photoresponse region. Moreover, OV on BiOBr (001) provides abundant coordination unsaturated sites for the reliable chemisorption of O2 in a terminal end-on structure [62], which extracts electrons from OV to form H2O2 via two-electron reduction. On the other hand, the N-CQDs can act as electron sinks to promote efficient migration of electrons from the BiOBr surface, which validly suppresses the recombination of photogenerated charges. Afterward, these collected electrons react with the dissolved oxygen adsorbed on N-CQDs to form O2 − through single-electron reduction. Partial O2 − participates into the further H2O2 production process, and the other part together with h+ in the VB of BiOBr directly oxidizes TC molecules. Meanwhile, the photogenerated H2O2 undergoes a Photo-Fenton like reaction to be dissociated into OH with powerful oxidizability towards TC pollutant.

While upon the illumination of NIR, BiOBr could not be excited directly because of its limited absorbance, however, the creation of oxygen vacancy extends the light absorption range of BiOBr to NIR region. Additionally, the N-CQDs could absorb the low energetic photons and then convert them into higher energy photons in the region of about 400 to 600 nm via upconversion. The fluorescence emissions will be in turn fully utilized by OV-BiOBr. In consequence, the synergistic broadening of optical window achieves the unprecedented enhancement for the photocatalytic decomposition of TC under NIR light irradiation.

In summary, a novel N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr nanocomposites with broad spectrum response ability was reported for the first time. The N-CQDs and oxygen vacancy were successfully anchored on the surface of BiOBr via a facile one-pot method. Compared to pristine BiOBr, enhanced photocatalytic degradation of TC under UV, visible and NIR light was achieved. The synergistic effect of N-CQDs and oxygen vacancy significantly widened the photoresponse region and efficiently steered the charge flow. Moreover, N-CQDs and oxygen vacancy were found to facilitate the generation of O2 − and H2O2 via single-electron and two-electron oxygen reduction, respectively, which benefitted the TC photodegradation by direct oxidization and indirect Photo-Fenton like reaction. Experiments in different conditions and water matrices proved that the N-CQDs/OV-BiOBr could be effectively applied to the degradation of TC under ambient water conditions. This work is of significance for providing efficient strategies to design highly efficient broad-spectrum response metallic oxide photocatalysts and build an ultra-efficient energy coupling between photons and oxygen-related radicals.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
